name: PAS
structure: State the exact pain → make it viscerally worse → present product as the only exit
copy_notes: Open with the pain in their own words. Agitate by naming the long-term cost of staying stuck. Introduce the product as relief, not a pitch. Use market language directly from research.
visual_style: Diagonal or vertical split. LEFT side (50%): dark, cold, oppressive — stressed person at a corporate desk, dim fluorescent lighting, muted gray-blue tones. RIGHT side (50%): bright, warm, aspirational — free, confident person in open space, golden light. Heavy bold headline overlays the dark left side.
best_for: Cold audiences who feel the pain daily

name: BAB
structure: Life before (visceral, recognisable) → life after (specific with numbers) → product is the bridge
copy_notes: Before must be so accurate it feels like reading their diary. After must use specific numbers (€10K/month, 3 clients, 30 days). The bridge is the mechanism — keep it one sentence.
visual_style: Two-panel split. LEFT panel labeled 'VOOR' (before): fully desaturated/grayscale, stressed person trapped in corporate setting. RIGHT panel labeled 'NA' (after): vivid warm colors, confident free person. A visual bridge element (diagonal stripe or arrow) crosses the dividing line. Transformation numbers visible.
best_for: Aspirational cold audiences who can visualise the outcome

name: Social Proof
structure: Most impressive specific number or testimonial → why this result is possible for viewer → CTA
copy_notes: Lead with the most specific number available (40+ clients, €10K/month, 5 stars). Explain why the result is achievable for the viewer specifically. CTA is low-friction.
visual_style: Clean review card layout. White card centered on contrasting background. ★★★★★ five gold stars prominent at top. Large specific number ('40+ klanten') in bold. Short testimonial quote in display type. Person name or photo if available. NOT a lifestyle photo — this must look like a real review.
best_for: Warm MOF audiences who need social validation

name: Direct Offer
structure: Bold promise → proof → specific offer with price → guarantee → CTA
copy_notes: No warm-up. Lead with the biggest promise you can prove. Price is visible. Guarantee front and centre. CTA is direct action. Only deploy to warm audiences who already know the product.
visual_style: High contrast dark background. Price or guarantee badge prominent. Bold headline with direct promise. Minimal supporting elements — the offer IS the visual. Premium, confident, decisive aesthetic.
best_for: BOF warm retargeting audiences ready to decide

name: Listicle
structure: Number in headline creates a promise → 3–5 reasons each addressing a different objection → last reason is the guarantee → CTA
copy_notes: Number in headline creates a commitment. Each list item must address a different objection or desire. Build momentum: weak → strong → strongest. Last item is always the guarantee.
visual_style: Numbered list with bold visual hierarchy. Clear numbered items (1, 2, 3...) with short bold points. Checkmark or number icons. Clean white or light background. Headline at top. Product or logo in corner. Reads top to bottom logically.
best_for: Analytical decision-makers who want all the reasons before deciding

name: Question Hook
structure: Mind-reading question in their exact vocabulary → validate the frustration → product as the logical answer → CTA
copy_notes: The question must feel like it reads their mind. Use exact vocabulary from customer voice research. The answer validates before introducing the product. Native content aesthetic — doesn't look like an ad.
visual_style: Clean, editorial, native-content feel. Large provocative question in display type dominates the visual. Minimal supporting imagery — the words ARE the visual. Clean background. Looks like organic content, not a traditional ad.
best_for: Sceptical audiences who scroll past traditional ads

name: Comparison
structure: Old way (what they're doing now) vs new way (the product approach) → new way wins on every dimension → CTA
copy_notes: Old way = the system they're currently trapped in. New way = the product mechanism. Never name competitors — describe the situation. Each comparison point addresses a specific objection.
visual_style: Two-column table or split. LEFT column: gray/muted, crossed-out items, ✗ marks, labeled 'Old Way' or 'Loondienst'. RIGHT column: branded colors, checkmarks ✓, labeled 'New Way' or product name. Clean structured layout. Visual contrast is stark.
best_for: Comparison shoppers who are evaluating alternatives

name: Result First
structure: Show the most impressive result upfront → explain the mechanism briefly → CTA
copy_notes: Lead with the most impressive specific result: €10K/month, first client in 30 days, €600 recovered in one deal. The 'how' is the mechanism — one sentence, credible. Result must be real and provable.
visual_style: Large bold result number as the dominant visual element — fills most of the frame. Supporting copy minimal. Clean premium background. The number IS the headline. Everything else is secondary. High contrast, confident.
best_for: Result-oriented audiences who want proof before everything

name: Empathy
structure: Demonstrate complete understanding of their pain → show you've been there → introduce solution as relief → CTA
copy_notes: Open with language they use internally — burned out, trapped, invisible. Show complete understanding before offering anything. Solution enters as a logical relief, not a pitch. Warm, human, conversational tone.
visual_style: Warm, human, authentic aesthetic. Expressive empathetic person in frame — not a model, a real human moment. Soft warm lighting. Conversational typography. Feels raw and genuine, not polished stock photography.
best_for: Burned-out audiences in early awareness who need to feel understood first

name: Bold Statement
structure: Surprising, polarising claim → immediate proof → CTA
copy_notes: The statement must be genuinely surprising — something they've never heard framed this way. Proof must follow immediately and be solid. No fluff. The bold claim creates the scroll-stop; proof creates the click.
visual_style: Maximum typographic impact. The bold statement IS the entire visual — full-bleed text on high-contrast background. Minimal or no photography. Heavy display font, maximum size. The words punch you in the face. Nothing competes with the text.
best_for: Pattern-interrupt resistant audiences who have seen every traditional ad

name: Sticky Note
structure: Handwritten or casual note-style message → personal insight or tip → CTA
copy_notes: Feels like a note from a friend, not a brand. Casual, personal language. Short sentences. Can be a tip, a revelation, or a personal confession. The handwritten/casual style signals authenticity.
visual_style: Sticky note visual (yellow, pastel pink, or white). Handwritten or casual sans-serif font. Simple, slightly imperfect layout. Plain or slightly textured background. Optional: multiple sticky notes layered. Feels organic, not designed.
best_for: Authenticity-seeking audiences who distrust polished marketing

name: iPhone Notes
structure: Authentic-looking iPhone Notes app screenshot → insight, confession, or story → CTA
copy_notes: Must look exactly like a real iPhone Notes screenshot. The content should be a personal insight, a list of thoughts, or a mini-story. No corporate language. First-person, raw, honest. Date and time visible in UI make it feel real.
visual_style: Pixel-perfect iPhone Notes app interface. White background, black/gray text in Apple's SF Pro font. Yellow folder icon if visible. Title in bold. Body text in the exact Notes app style. Screen held in hand (optional) to increase authenticity.
best_for: Audiences who respond to authentic, platform-native content

name: iMessage
structure: Text conversation showing a transformation, insight, or recommendation → CTA
copy_notes: Conversation must feel 100% real. Blue bubbles (sender) and gray bubbles (receiver). The conversation reveals the key value proposition naturally through dialogue. Can be a friend recommendation, a question answered, or a transformation revealed.
visual_style: Authentic iMessage UI. iPhone frame (optional). Blue sent bubbles, gray received bubbles. Apple system font. Time stamps. Read receipts. The conversation content is the ad — it must look exactly like a real text thread.
best_for: Audiences who engage with conversational, peer-recommendation content

name: ChatGPT Ad
structure: ChatGPT-style conversation revealing a key insight about the product or problem → CTA
copy_notes: Prompt should be something the target audience would genuinely ask ChatGPT. The AI response validates the product approach or reveals the market truth. Feels like the viewer discovered this themselves.
visual_style: ChatGPT dark interface. User prompt in white box at bottom. AI response in the main area with ChatGPT styling. Rendered exactly like the real ChatGPT UI. Dark mode (#212121 background). The conversation IS the entire visual.
best_for: Tech-comfortable audiences who trust AI-generated insights

name: Us vs Them
structure: Our approach vs their approach → we win on every dimension the viewer cares about → CTA
copy_notes: Never name the competitor — describe the category. 'Us' column highlights what makes the product uniquely valuable. 'Them' column calls out the frustrations the avatar already has. Be specific, not vague.
visual_style: Two clearly labeled columns: branded column (left or right) with ✓ checkmarks, competitor/old-way column with ✗ marks. Brand colors on 'us' side, gray/neutral on 'them' side. Bold header. Clean table structure. Decisive visual contrast.
best_for: Decision-stage audiences actively comparing options

name: Benefit Callout
structure: Single most compelling benefit front and centre → why this benefit matters → secondary benefits → CTA
copy_notes: One benefit must dominate. Choose the benefit that addresses the single biggest objection. Secondary benefits can list 2-3 supporting points. Clarity wins over cleverness here.
visual_style: Clean, confident minimal layout. One large benefit statement dominates. Supporting benefit icons or bullet points below. Lots of white space. Product visible. Premium, uncluttered. Like an Apple ad — the less, the stronger.
best_for: Warm audiences who need a clear, specific reason to act

name: UGC Static
structure: Raw authentic user/client testimonial or result → context → why this matters for the viewer → CTA
copy_notes: Must not look like an ad. Authentic photo quality (slightly imperfect). First-person language from a real-sounding person. Specific result with name and context. Feels like organic social content that happened to go viral.
visual_style: Raw, authentic photo or selfie-style image. Not studio quality — real-world lighting, natural setting. Person looking genuine, not posed. Text overlay in casual font. Looks like something a satisfied customer would post on Instagram or TikTok.
best_for: Ad-fatigued audiences who skip polished ads but engage with authentic content

name: Cartoon Style
structure: Illustrated scene showing the problem or transformation → clear message → CTA
copy_notes: Illustration stands out in a feed of photography. The cartoon style makes difficult topics approachable. Story told visually through characters. Simpler, more memorable message. Works well for cold audiences who need pattern interruption.
visual_style: Custom illustrated style. Bold outlines, flat or slightly textured colours. Character-driven scene. Exaggerated expressions. Clear visual story. NOT clipart — high-quality editorial cartoon or modern flat illustration. Distinctive and memorable.
best_for: Cold audiences who scroll past photography but stop for illustrated content

name: Lifestyle Context
structure: Aspirational lifestyle scene with product naturally integrated → implication of what this life feels like → CTA
copy_notes: The lifestyle IS the message. Product integrated naturally — not held up for display. The scene should show the life the avatar desires: freedom, success, flexibility. Copy is minimal — the visual does the heavy lifting.
visual_style: Premium lifestyle photography. Real-world aspirational setting (home office, laptop in a cafe, standing on a balcony). Natural lighting, authentic moment. Product/laptop/phone naturally in scene. Minimal text overlay. Feels like a premium brand editorial shoot.
best_for: Aspirational audiences motivated by identity and lifestyle transformation

name: Carousel Ad
structure: Frame 1: provocative hook that demands a swipe → Frames 2-4: build the case step by step → Final frame: CTA with offer
copy_notes: Frame 1 must demand a swipe with unresolved tension. Each subsequent frame reveals more. Final frame delivers the offer and CTA. Visual continuity between frames. The first frame IS the most important — it determines whether anyone sees the rest.
visual_style: First slide of a carousel series. Right edge of frame shows a partial next slide to signal 'swipe'. Bold hook headline. Clear visual continuation cue (arrow or partial reveal). Branded design system consistent across all frames. Static representation of the first frame.
best_for: Engaged audiences who will invest time in multi-frame storytelling

name: Review
structure: Specific review from a real customer → the result they got → why they recommend it → CTA
copy_notes: The review must be specific and believable — not generic praise. Include the person's name, context, and the specific result they achieved. Platform badge (Google, Trustpilot) adds credibility. Star rating must be visible and prominent.
visual_style: Google Review or Trustpilot card aesthetic. Platform logo and rating stars at top. Reviewer photo (circle, if available) and name. Review text in readable font. Verified badge. Clean white card on contrasting background. Looks exactly like a screenshot of a real review platform.
best_for: Trust-driven audiences who research before buying

name: Negative/Positive
structure: Show the negative reality now (pain, cost, frustration) → contrast immediately with the positive alternative → CTA
copy_notes: Negative side must be viscerally real — not exaggerated. Use exact language from customer voice research. Positive side must be specific and credible. The contrast creates the emotional shift that drives action. Red/dark vs green/bright colour coding.
visual_style: Split design. LEFT side: red or dark tones, ✗ marks, frustrated/stressed visual, negative situation spelled out in text. RIGHT side: green or bright tones, ✓ checkmarks, positive outcome, aspirational. Bold contrast between the two halves. Colour coding is critical — red = bad, green = good.
best_for: Pain-aware audiences who need to clearly see the cost of inaction vs the reward of acting
